chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter

phi.name: work function
phi.note: Minimum energy needed to eject electron from metal surface; varies by material (Na ≈ 2.3 eV, Cu ≈ 4.7 eV)

f0.name: threshold frequency
f0.note: Minimum frequency below which no photoelectrons are emitted regardless of intensity

V0.name: stopping potential
V0.note: Minimum negative potential needed to stop the most energetic photoelectron

I.name: intensity of incident light
I.note: Affects number of photoelectrons (photocurrent) but NOT their maximum kinetic energy

locked_facts: E = hf — energy of a photon is Planck's constant times frequency
KE_max = hf - φ — Einstein's photoelectric equation; maximum KE equals photon energy minus work function
f₀ = φ/h — threshold frequency; below this frequency no emission occurs regardless of intensity
eV₀ = hf - φ — stopping potential times electron charge equals photon energy minus work function
Photoelectron emission is instantaneous (< 10⁻⁹ s) once f ≥ f₀, regardless of how weak the light is
Photocurrent is proportional to intensity of incident light (number of photons per second)
KE_max depends only on frequency, not on intensity — each photon interacts with one electron
Graph of V₀ vs f is a straight line with slope h/e and x-intercept at f₀
truth_statements: Below threshold frequency f₀, no electrons are emitted no matter how intense the light — this disproved the wave theory of light for this phenomenon
Increasing intensity increases the number of emitted photoelectrons (photocurrent) but does not change their maximum kinetic energy
The photoelectric effect is instantaneous — there is no observable time lag between illumination and electron emission when f ≥ f₀
The slope of the stopping potential vs frequency graph is h/e, which is the same for all metals; only the intercept (determined by φ) changes
Einstein received the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics for explaining the photoelectric effect using the quantum theory of light

aha_moment.description: Student sees that doubling intensity doubles the photocurrent but KE_max stays fixed, while even a tiny increase in frequency above f₀ immediately increases KE_max — proving energy is quantized in photons, not spread continuously as a wave
aha_moment.visual_cue: Two side-by-side graphs: intensity slider moves photocurrent curve up/down but KE_max line stays flat; frequency slider shifts KE_max linearly upward

STATE_1.title: Photon Hits Metal Surface
STATE_1.description: Show monochromatic light (photons as discrete packets) hitting a metal surface. Display the photon energy E = hf and the work function φ of the metal. When f < f₀, photons bounce off — no electrons emitted despite high intensity.

STATE_2.title: Threshold Frequency Crossed
STATE_2.description: Increase frequency past f₀. Electrons start ejecting immediately. Show the energy accounting: photon energy hf splits into work function φ (used to escape) and kinetic energy KE_max (leftover). Instantaneous emission emphasized.

STATE_3.title: Intensity vs Frequency — The Key Distinction
STATE_3.description: Split view: LEFT panel shows intensity slider (more photons → more electrons, but same KE_max). RIGHT panel shows frequency slider (higher f → higher KE_max, same number of electrons per photon). V₀ vs f graph plotted in real-time.

STATE_4.title: Interactive Exploration
STATE_4.description: Student controls frequency, intensity, and metal type (φ). Real-time updates to photocurrent, KE_max, and stopping potential. Graphs update live. Challenge: find the threshold frequency for each metal.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Increasing the intensity of light increases the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons
common_misconceptions.correct: Intensity increases the NUMBER of photoelectrons (photocurrent) but NOT their KE_max. KE_max depends only on frequency.

common_misconceptions.misconception: There should be a time delay for dim light to accumulate enough energy to eject an electron
common_misconceptions.correct: Emission is instantaneous because energy is delivered in discrete photon packets, not spread as a continuous wave. One photon with f ≥ f₀ is enough.
trigger_phrases: kam light mein time lagta hai

common_misconceptions.misconception: If I use very high intensity light below threshold frequency, electrons will eventually be emitted
common_misconceptions.correct: No emission occurs below f₀ regardless of intensity. Each photon individually lacks sufficient energy (hf < φ), and one electron absorbs only one photon in the standard photoelectric effect.

legend.photon: Yellow arrow/packet representing a quantum of light energy E = hf
legend.electron: Blue dot ejected from metal surface with kinetic energy KE_max
legend.work_function_bar: Red horizontal line showing minimum energy φ required to free an electron
legend.KE_max_bar: Green bar showing leftover energy after overcoming work function
legend.V0_graph: Straight line graph of stopping potential vs frequency with slope h/e

concept_relationships.relationship: Photoelectrons have de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p = h/√(2mKE_max)
Photon energy levels in Bohr model produce discrete emission spectra; same photon concept applies to photoelectric effect
Light as electromagnetic wave has frequency and wavelength related by c = fλ; photoelectric effect reveals its particle nature
KE_max = hf - φ is an energy conservation equation analogous to work-energy theorem
